study_info.title: Medullary thyroid cancer in a 9-week-old infant with familial MEN 2B: Implications for timing of prophylactic thyroidectomy
study_info.ethics_approval: Not stated; written informed consent was obtained from the patient’s mother for publication

ATA_risk_categories.description: Highest risk category for aggressive MTC

genotype_phenotype_notes: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2 (MEN 2) is an autosomal dominant hereditary cancer syndrome with a prevalence of about 1 in 30,000.
Germline mutations in the RET proto-oncogene account for MEN 2A, MEN 2B and familial medullary thyroid cancer.
All MEN 2 syndromes are associated with medullary thyroid carcinoma, which frequently metastasizes and is the primary MEN-related cause of death.
MEN 2B is the rarest and most aggressive form of MEN 2, associated with early-onset MTC, pheochromocytoma, mucosal neuromas, intestinal ganglioneuromatosis and a Marfanoid habitus.
Strong genotype-phenotype correlations enable risk stratification and recommendations for timing of prophylactic thyroidectomy.

genetic_assays: RET proto-oncogene mutation analysis for M918T
guidelines_referenced: American Thyroid Association medullary thyroid cancer management guidelines (2009)
Guidelines for diagnosis and therapy of MEN type 1 and type 2

clinical_findings: A full-term male infant with familial MEN 2B had an M918T RET proto-oncogene mutation confirmed at 4 weeks of age.
He underwent prophylactic total thyroidectomy at 9 weeks of age; pathology showed a 2.5 mm calcitonin-positive nodule consistent with microscopic MTC.
There was no vascular invasion and no visible or palpable lymph nodes at surgery.
Post-operatively, serum calcitonin declined from 81 pg/mL at 4 weeks to 8 pg/mL and has remained in this range.
At 2 years of age, carcinoembryonic antigen was 2.1 ng/mL (normal <5 ng/mL) and thyroglobulin was undetectable, with no residual thyroid tissue or lymph node enlargement on ultrasound.

The child has normal growth and development but has chronic constipation due to biopsy-proven intestinal ganglioneuromatosis, absent tearing, and a tongue nodule likely representing a mucosal neuroma.

The mother presented at 13 years of age with mucosal neuromas, a typical Marfanoid habitus and MTC, underwent total thyroidectomy and lymph node dissection with multifocal MTC and multiple lymph node metastases.
The mother later developed hepatic and pulmonary metastases and underwent resection of a pheochromocytoma at age 25 years.
At 27 years of age, the mother is alive and well but has persistent MTC with serum calcitonin concentration over 2000 pg/mL.
guideline_related_results: Current ATA guidelines state that infants with ATA-D mutations (MEN 2B) should undergo prophylactic thyroidectomy as soon as possible and within the first year of life in an experienced tertiary care setting.
Former consensus guidelines recommended thyroidectomy within the first six months and preferably within the first month of life.
The reported case and prior reports show that MTC can present as early as 2–3 months of age, supporting early prophylactic surgery during infancy.
The authors suggest that in familial cases, when the diagnosis is anticipated, thyroidectomy soon after the first month of life could be considered.

Figure_1.title_or_caption: A thyroidectomy specimen of the patient reveals a 2.5 mm, poorly defined tumor nodule with focal infiltrative borders. The tumor cells are relatively uniform with moderately eosinophilic cytoplasm, and round to slightly irregular nuclei with open chromatin (1A and inset). The tumor cells show strong reactivity to calcitonin immunohistochemical staining (1B).
Figure_1.description: Histologic images of the infant’s thyroidectomy specimen showing microscopic medullary thyroid carcinoma with calcitonin-positive tumor cells.

Figure_2.title_or_caption: Suggested algorithm to facilitate early thyroidectomy during infancy.
Figure_2.description: Schematic algorithm proposing prenatal counseling, early genetic testing, prenatal surgical referral and scheduling, and thyroidectomy soon after 30 days of age in familial MEN 2B.

discussion_notes: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2 (MEN 2) has a prevalence of about 1 in 30,000.
Prophylactic thyroidectomy is recommended in asymptomatic patients with pathogenic RET mutations before development of medullary thyroid carcinoma.
MEN 2B is the rarest and most aggressive form of MEN 2 and is associated with early-onset MTC, pheochromocytoma, mucosal neuromas, intestinal ganglioneuromas and a Marfanoid habitus.
More than 95% of MEN 2B cases have an M918T RET mutation in exon 16, and 2–3% have an A883F mutation; both are classified as ATA-D, the highest risk category for aggressive MTC.
The reported infant represents one of the youngest patients with biopsy-proven MTC at 9 weeks of age associated with familial MEN 2B.
Microscopic MTC in this infant was confirmed by a 2.5 mm tumor with infiltrating borders and calcitonin reactivity, distinguishing it from C-cell hyperplasia.
Other early MTC reports in MEN 2B include microscopic MTC in a 9-week-old with a de novo mutation and metastatic MTC in a 3-month-old infant with familial MEN 2B.
The benefit of prophylactic thyroidectomy before onset of metastatic MTC is well accepted.
ATA guidelines state that prophylactic thyroidectomy for ATA-D mutations must be undertaken as soon as possible and within the first year of life.
The case raises the question of whether intervention should occur as soon as possible in earlier infancy rather than waiting up to 1 year.
In familial MEN 2B, where diagnosis is anticipated, thyroidectomy soon after the first month of life could be considered.

Current barriers to early prophylactic thyroidectomy include delays in genetic diagnosis and surgical scheduling, concerns about surgical and anesthetic risks in early infancy, and concerns about iatrogenic hypothyroidism.
Early genetic testing of at-risk infants soon after birth or prenatally is recommended to avoid treatment delays.

ATA guidelines recommend prenatal genetic counseling for all patients with MEN 2B of child-bearing age, including discussion of pre-implantation genetic testing, chorionic villus sampling and amniocentesis.
Prenatal referral and anticipatory scheduling of thyroidectomy in familial cases could make postnatal genetic confirmation the only rate-limiting step.
Pre-operative calcitonin measurement is recommended, but in infants under 6 months values should be interpreted with caution and should not dictate surgical decisions.
Children younger than six years have the highest complication rate after thyroidectomy compared with adults, but outcomes may improve with high-volume surgeons.
Significant delay of prophylactic thyroidectomy is not an option in MEN 2B, where surgery has to be undertaken by 1 year of age and possibly earlier.

There are no formal studies of thyroidectomy outcomes in infants under 1 year; the authors opine that surgical risk at 1 month is not much greater than at 6 months when performed by experienced pediatric thyroid surgeons with appropriate resources.

Post-operative hypoparathyroidism in children with MEN does not appear related to age at surgery, while both hypoparathyroidism and recurrent laryngeal nerve injury risks are increased by lymph node dissection.
Thyroidectomy before lymph node metastases obviates the need for central compartment lymph node dissection, which is associated with higher rates of hypoparathyroidism and vocal cord paralysis.
General anesthesia risk is greater in children under 1 year of age, but can be attenuated by screening for prematurity, congenital heart disease and other congenital abnormalities.
The authors typically wait until a full-term infant is 1 month of age before elective anesthesia to minimize postoperative apnea, and suggest thyroid surgery after 30 days of age in MEN 2B.
Concerns about undertreating iatrogenic hypothyroidism should not delay thyroidectomy; instead, intensive monitoring and careful levothyroxine replacement are essential.

The authors propose a practical algorithm to facilitate timely prophylactic thyroidectomy in early infancy for familial MEN 2B, including prenatal counseling, prenatal surgical referral and scheduling, and postnatal surgery soon after 1 month if RET testing is positive.
Ideally, surgery should be performed at a tertiary care center by an experienced pediatric thyroid surgeon with pediatric anesthesiology support and skilled postoperative pediatric nursing.
Although prophylactic thyroidectomy in inherited MEN 2B is a pediatric problem, adult endocrinologists and obstetricians often serve as gatekeepers for referral and timely intervention.
Early collaboration between adult and pediatric specialists is key to optimizing management of infants with familial MEN 2B.
